3. Random Variables

3.1. Definition and Examples

Definition 7 (Random Variables). Let be a measurable space.

  1. A function is a random variable if the set is -measurable for every , i.e. .
  2. A function is an extended-value random variable if the set is -measurable for every .

More generally, given a function is -measurable if the preimage of all Borel sets is measurable: for all Borel sets in , the Borel -algebra on . It suffices to check the family of half intervals , which is a standard generator for .

Informally, we define a random variable to admit a CDF. Denote a random variable as uppercase , and its realization as lowercase . We abbreviate the event as .

Examples:

  • , is a random variable since .
  • Indicator functions: , equals 1 iff . If , then is a random variable, as the preimage of is and the preimage of is . Example: .
  • Suppose is a RV and let . The set equals the set . Hence is a RV. More generally, functions of RV(s) are a RV.
  • Real analysis: Let be a sequence of RVs. Is a RV? Example: . Then for otherwise 1. What about lim and lim sup? See theorem below.
  • Important: Normal distribution is realized as the identity RV on the pushforward measure on .
Proposition 4. Any continuous map is measurable with respect to the Borel σ-algebra.
Proof

Continuity implies that preimages of open sets are open.

□

Theorem 6 (Common RVs).

  • Indicator function is measurable.
  • Simple random variables are measurable.
  • Suppose that and that is continuous. Then is a random variable.
  • If is continuous (or more generally, -measurable). Then is a random variable.
  • Continuous functions of multiple random variables are measurable. For example, the sum and product of RVs is an RV.
Proof

For the last one I think you use the fact that intersections of measuable sets is measurable. Or define the valued RV and use the fact that preimages is well behaved under composition.

□

Theorem 7 (Real analytic RVs). Let be a measurable space. If is a sequence of RVs, then , , and are RVs. If the sequence converges pointwise to , then is also a random variable.

Definition 8 (Probability Law of a RV). Let be a probability space, and let be a random variable.

For every Borel subset of the real line, we define

(19)

The resulting function is called the probability law of .

Proposition 5 (Pushforward measure). Let be a probability space and be a RV. Then is a probability measure on . This relates the probability space encoding the underlying outcomes to that of the observed values.
Proof

We have and It suffices to verify countable additivity. Let be a countable sequence of disjoint Borel subsets of . Then the sets are also disjoint. Then apply countable additivity on the original probability space:

(20)

□

Note. The RV defined by the identity map is a random variable that has the same distribution up to null sets.

3.2. CDF

Definition 9 (CDF). Let be a random variable. The function , defined by

(21)

is called CDF of .

Proposition 6 (Properties of CDF). Let be a RV, and let be its CDF.

  1. (Monotonicity) If , then .
  2. (Limiting values) We have and .
  3. (Right-continuity) For every , we have
Proof

(a) is trivial. (b) and (c) follow from continuity of probabilities.

□

Remarks.

  • Left-continuity doesn’t follow from the definition due to the sign (For , we have .
  • Right continuity allows for sudden jumps in the CDF. A continuous distribution has a differentiable CDF4, and a discrete distribution has a step function CDF. A mixed distribution has both features.

A function that satisfies those three properties (call it a distribution function) also admits a RV by the following theorem. Hence there is a bijection between distribution functions and random variables.

Theorem 8 (Every distribution function is the realization of an RV and hence a CDF). Let be a given distribution function. Consider the probability space , where is the Borel -field, and is the Lebesgue measure. There exists a measurable function whose CDF satisfies .

That is, for any distribution function , we can construct a RV on the canonical uniform probability space whose CDF is .

Proof

We can construct it directly? Define the quantile function as as

(22)

and let so that

(23)

□

Informally, this the trick of sampling from a given CDF by defining an RV in and applying the inverse cdf (quantile function)

The CDF only specifies the probabilities of certain intervals. Nevertheless, it contains enough information to recover the probability law of (i.e. probabilities of Borel sets).

Proposition 7 (Bijection between probability law and CDF). The probability law of a random variable is uniquely determined by CDF .
Proof

[TODO] uses extension theorem.

□

To sample CDF, use uniform distribution and take transformation of variables.

3.3. Discrete RVs

A random variable , defined on a probability space is said to be discrete if its range is finite or countable.

Proposition 8 (Binomial convergence to Poisson). Fix some , and suppose that , for every . Let . Then as , the PMF of converges to the PMF of , in the sense that , for any .

  • Joint and conditional pmfs.

Definition 10 (Independence of random variables).

  1. Let be random variables defined on the same probability space. We say that RVs are independent if the events are independent for any Borel subsets of the real line. Namely,

    (24)

  2. A collection of random variables is independent if every finite subset of RVs is independent.
Proposition 9. The above definition is equivalent to checking only Borel sets of the form . I.e. the events are independent. Equivalently, the joint cdf is the product of their individual CDFs.

Theorem 9. Let be discrete random variables defined on the same probability space. The following are equivalent.

  1. and are independent.
  2. For any , the events and are independent.
  4. For any s.t. , we have .
  5. For any functions and , the random variables and are independent.

whenever the sum is well defined (see Section 1.2), and where the sum is taken over the countable set of values in the range of .

The sum is both well-defined and finite if the positivie and negative series convergence. This is the same condition as requiring , and we say that is integrable. A square integrable RV is always integrable because . More generally, th moment convergence implies smaller moments are also finite.

Example 1. Matching problem: suppose we take a random permutation. Let be the RV for how many people get their hats back. Compute expectation and variance and the PMF.

For pmf, we can compute pmf of n and pmf of zero (derangements), and then use that to compute pmf of r. In particular, if there must be a subset such that its fully returned and the other one is deranged. Can also compute using inclusion exclusion. The PMF corresponds to a Poisson distribution with parameter 1 as . Similar mechanism as why binomial PMF approaches the Poisson opmf.

3.4. Continuous RVs

In this section we develop continuous RVs using Riemann integration. Later we revisit it with abstract integration.

Definition 12 (Continuous RVs). A random variable , defined on a probability space is said to be continuous if there exists a nonnegative Lebesgue measurable function such that

(28)

is called the probability density function of (PDF).

Measurable here means that is a Borel set for all Borel sets . We require to be measurable because later we define integration only for measurable functions.

Any nonnegative measurable function that is normalized to 1 corresponds to a RV whose PDF is the given density. Howover, multiple PDFs can be associated to the same CDF—change the PDF at a finite set of points without affecting the integral.

Mixed distribution: Let be a discrete RV, and be a continuous RV. Fix , so that

(29)

is a distribution function for an RV that is neither discrete (there are a continuum values) nor continuous, (the PDF does not exist).

What if instead of 2 worlds ( and ), we sample for uncountablely many worlds? We get a singular distribution. See appendix.

3.4.3. Independence

Let and be jointly continuous random variables defined on the same probability space. The following are equivalent.

  1. and are independent (definition).
  2. and are independent for all .
  3. for all .
  4. The marginal and joint densities satisfy for all except possibly on a set that has Lebesgue measure zero.
